hook_type: Identity-then-FOMO: 'you do this, 137K do it as owners'
tactic: Customer-to-owner bridge with hard community number -> mechanism reveal -> recursion
primary_text:
You back Aussie brands every week, and 137,000 Australians own them too.

Buying Naked Life or Our Cow makes you a customer. Crowd-Sourced Funding lets you own a piece instead of just buying. 324 raises, $234M+, all from everyday investors, not VCs.

Now we are opening our own raise on Birchal.

Save your spot. Read the offer when it opens.

*Always consider the general CSF risk warning and offer document before investing.
headline: Australians who back Aussie brands
description: You buy from them already. Now you can own a piece.
_v4_changes: Hook rewrite: 'You back Aussie brands when you shop. 137,000 Australians do it with ownership too.' -> 'You back Aussie brands every week, and 137,000 Australians own them too.' Single sentence (was two) so the full first sentence scores Halbert 5/5 in --primary-text mode, where V3's first sentence alone scored 4. 'every week' makes the behaviour concrete; 'own them too' is the FOMO punch.

Para 2 tightened: explicit customer-vs-owner contrast ('makes you a customer ... own a piece instead of just buying') replaces the looser V3 'you are choosing Australian-made' framing. Adds 'not VCs' to the cadence-of-three proof for the no-gatekeeper note.
Cut to 72 words (V3 A2 was 71). Kept the winning belonging DNA (shop -> own bridge, 137,000 anchor, brand grid) and the 'own a piece' ownership phrase the FMTH evidence flags as load-bearing.
_drc_audience: B2C considered, solution-aware. First-time CSF investors who self-identify as backing Australian-made.
_compliance_findings_resolved: PREOFFER-VERBS: 'save your spot', 'read', 'own a piece'. No transaction verbs.
VERBATIM-OFFER-LANGUAGE: 'Read the offer when it opens'. No 'we will send'.
VERBATIM-CSF-WARNING: byte-exact final line, appears exactly once.
BRAND-EXCLUDED: only verified-good raises (Naked Life RAISE-001, Our Cow RAISE-002).
Subject discipline: 'Birchal' once; first-person we/our elsewhere.
